methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $640M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Redwood Materials
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series D), post-money $6B+

data.narrative:
Solugen, a Series D climate and energy technology company that has raised $640M+, is valued using a comparable universe of 9 private and 1 public company peers operating in the same sector and stage. The peer set includes Redwood Materials, KoBold Metals, Electric Hydrogen, and Amogy, with valuations ranging from $700M to $6.0B.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression, anchoring to $640M in total capital raised and applying a 1.5x multiplier typical of Series D advancement, implies a valuation of $960M. Peer-median analysis across four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $2.0B, with a distribution spanning $700M at the low end and $6.0B at the high end. The wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in technology maturity and commercialization stage within the climate tech cohort.

Triangulating these two methods produces a valuation range of $816M (low) to $2.3B (high), with a median point estimate of $1.5B. The stage-progression method anchors to capitalized funding and yields the lower bound, while peer-median pulls toward higher multiples reflective of recent private market appetite for climate infrastructure plays. Key uncertainties include technology differentiation relative to Redwood Materials—the highest-valued peer at $6.0B—and the timing and terms of potential exit pathways. The $1.5B median reflects balanced application of both methods given limited data granularity on company-specific unit economics.
